heading: OTA EDITORIALE
card_summary: This chunk contains two distinct sections. The first is Campanini's editorial note (Nota editoriale) detailing his text-critical methodology for the Bompiani edition: he used the editio princeps (Venice, 1525, sigla A) as base text, collated against the second edition (1545, sigla B), standardized punctuation, preserved authorial capitalization anomalies, and added marginal page references for both editions. He also documents a significant pre-publication variant in the 2008 La Finestra anastatic reprint (from a Biblioteca Nazionale Centrale di Firenze copy), involving a cancelled passage at c. LXXVIIIr concerning the Hebrew word paz (Song of Songs 5:15) and the reading "charitate" vs. "claritate." The second section opens Zorzi's Proemio generale (General Preface), addressed to Pope Francis, where Zorzi invokes the image of gleaning from the Lord's harvest to justify his project as ancient wisdom in new form. He then launches an anti-Aristotelian epistemological argument: syllogistic reasoning rooted in fallible sense-perception cannot reach divine truth, citing Proclus on the incomprehensibility of the Creator's nature, Pyrrho of Elis on sense-deception, Plato on divine oracles, Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite on the limits of ascent to divine knowledge, and examples from the Brahmins and Indian sages (via Philostratus on Pharaotes) and Lysis the Pythagorean writing to Hipparchus, all converging on the necessity of soul-purification before philosophical initiation.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 38 straddles two distinct textual units. Pages 166–169 contain Campanini's **Nota editoriale**, the technical statement of his editorial principles for the Bompiani critical edition. Pages 170–173 open Zorzi's own **Proemio generale** — the general preface to *De Harmonia Mundi*, addressed to the pope — which establishes the epistemological and spiritual premises of the entire work. The juxtaposition is instructive: the editor's philological rigor precedes and frames the theologian's mystical prolegomena.

---

## Campanini's Editorial Note (pp. 166–169)

### Base Text and Collation

Campanini explains that the Latin text reproduces the *editio princeps* of 1525 (sigla **A**), systematically collated with the second edition of 1545 (sigla **B**). The 1564 edition is excluded because it merely reprints B with a new title page. Marginal references give page numbers of both editions — Roman numerals for A, Arabic for B — enabling verification of citations regardless of which edition a reader consults.

Campanini standardized punctuation, reducing the frequent use of the colon for readability, but deliberately refrained from regularizing capitalization or spelling, recognizing that apparent inconsistencies in Zorzi's use of majuscules often reflect deliberate authorial intent.

### The 2008 La Finestra Reprint and its Variants

A notable section concerns the 2008 anastatic reprint published by La Finestra, based on a copy held at the Biblioteca Nazionale Centrale di Firenze (shelf mark Guicciardini 12.1.14) — a copy that had escaped the heavy censorship applied to most surviving exemplars. Campanini identifies this as a textually significant "pre-publication proof with variants." The copy presents two notable anomalies:

1. Folios XXXVr and XXXIXv are each printed twice (in their correct positions and also displacing folios XLr and XXXVIIv respectively), with those displaced folios missing entirely.
2. Folios LXXVr, LXXVv, and LXXVIIIv were evidently reset in type for the standard edition.

Most importantly, at c. LXXVIIIr (second numeration), the reprint preserves a passage that Zorzi apparently cancelled during proof-correction. Campanini quotes it:

> *Et hae virtutes fundatae sunt super bases aureas, idest affectus charitate inflammatos, et non argenteas, ut habet traductio nostra. Habetur enim in textu hal adne paz. Nec enim illud paz pro argento a sapientibus aliquando interpretatum vidi, sed pro auro purissimo.*

<cite>p. 167, cancelled variant at c. LXXVIIIr of the La Finestra reprint</cite>

The passage concerns the Hebrew word **paz** from *Song of Songs* 5:15 ("hal adne paz"), which Zorzi interprets as "purest gold" rather than "silver." Campanini notes that the Hebrew text as transliterated in the variant is actually erroneous (the correct word for gold is *ketem*, not what appears), and he observes that the reading *charitate* ("inflamed by charity") in place of *claritate* ("brightness") — found in both A and B — is philologically preferable. Campanini declines to speculate on Zorzi's reasons for suppressing the variant.

### The Errata Corrige

The apparatus also records the corrections from the *Errata corrige* appended to A, introduced by the words:

> *Recognito diligenter toto volumine Candidissime Lector, errores, qui alicuius momenti visi sunt, collegi. Si quos vero accuratius legendo reppereris tuipse, si libuerit, emendabis.*

<cite>p. 168, from the Errata corrige of editio princeps (1525)</cite>

---

## The Proemio Generale: Opening of Zorzi's Preface (pp. 170–173)

### Dedication and the Harvest Metaphor

The preface opens with a dedication to the pope (*Pater Beatissime*) and immediately deploys a rich agricultural metaphor: Zorzi presents himself as a gleaner (*spigolatore*) gathering the ears left behind by the great harvesters, working to carry a small portion of the Lord's abundant harvest into His granary to feed the poor. The grain he treads out is old, not new — ancient wisdom refashioned in a new form, watered not from exhausted cisterns but from the living spring of salvific wisdom. The allusion to John 4:14 (the water becoming a spring leaping up to eternal life) is explicit.

> *Non equidem Pater Beatissime fastu ullo, neque secundum carnem, et sanguinem, sed bono genio, immo et meliori spiritu ducor, ut pro minus collustratis hanc qualemcumque accendere coner faculam, non certe novi luminis, sed eam, quae a luce illa omnes illuminante subrutilat.*

<cite>p. 170 (Proemio generale, opening lines)</cite>

This rhetoric of modesty and secondary illumination — Zorzi's torch reflects another light, it does not originate one — is a programmatic statement: *De Harmonia Mundi* claims to recover and transmit the *prisca sapientia*, not to originate it.

### The Attack on Syllogistic Reasoning

The preface pivots swiftly to epistemology. Zorzi mounts a sustained critique of Aristotelian-scholastic method: syllogistic reasoning, being rooted in sensory experience, cannot reach divine truth. Three philosophical authorities underwrite this claim:

1. **Proclus** (*Proculus*): syllogistic deductions, however elaborate, cannot touch the "solitary and intellectual nature of the Creator" (*solitariam illam, atque intellectualem Opificis naturam*). This is a Neoplatonist epistemological limit — the One transcends discursive reason.

2. **Pyrrho of Elis** (*Pyrrho Heliates*): the senses themselves are deceptive, as "amply demonstrated" by the founder of ancient skepticism. Zorzi here enlists Academic/Pyrrhonist skepticism in service of a mystical conclusion: if the senses deceive, then sense-based reasoning yields no truth.

3. **Plato**: matters of divine truth must be compared to oracles (*oraculis comparari debent*) and cannot be handled by the methods applicable to sensible things. The Platonic oracle-knowledge is set against Aristotelian demonstration.

> *Sed eae deductiones compositae et in ipsis sensibus radicitus fundatae solitariam illam, atque intellectualem Opificis naturam (ut Proculus docet) attingere nequeunt.*

<cite>p. 171</cite>

### Pseudo-Dionysius and the Limit of Ascent

Zorzi invokes the **Areopagita** (Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite) to define the proper limit of theological ascent: one may only rise toward the divine to the extent that the "ray of divine eloquence" has penetrated and illuminated the purified mind. Divine things hate contention, desire silence, repel the proud and worldly-wise, embrace the humble and the little ones (*parvulos*), and laugh at syllogisms — for divinity has no principle prior to itself by which it could be demonstrated.

> *Hinc tantum ad divina, teste Areopagita, conscendere fas est, quantum seipsum divinorum eloquiorum radius insinuaverit, qui expurgatis mentibus sese percipiendum tantummodo offert.*

<cite>p. 172</cite>

### Soul-Purification: Brahmin, Indian, and Pythagorean Parallels

The preface closes this section with a comparative survey of initiation requirements across traditions, all pointing to the necessity of purifying the soul before approaching philosophical or divine wisdom:

- The **Brahmins** (*Brachmanae*) admitted no one to their wisdom unless they abstained from wine, meat, and above all vice, seeking to imitate God through divine conversation.
- **Pharaotes**, the Indian sage, taught the same among the "lower Indians" — attested by **Philostratus** (*si Philostrato credimus*).
- **Lysis the Pythagorean** (*Lysides Pythagoreus*), writing to Hipparchus, taught that it was impious to share the mysteries of true philosophy — which "breathes divinity" — with those incapable of even dreaming of soul-purification. The image comes from **Hierocles**: a bleary, unclean eye (*lippus et immundus oculus*) cannot gaze on things of excessive brightness.

The section ends with the image of someone newly admitted to sacred rites who, not yet fully initiated, cannot immediately perceive divine illuminations — just as one newly awakened from sleep (*experrectus a somno*) cannot immediately contemplate lucid things. The soul must be habituated to beautiful studies.

### Connections to the Larger Work

This proem establishes the epistemological architecture that governs *De Harmonia Mundi* as a whole: revealed wisdom (*prisca theologia*) mediated through Scripture, Kabbalah, and Neoplatonism supersedes scholastic demonstration; the path to cosmological knowledge requires moral and spiritual preparation; and the harmony Zorzi will describe is accessible only to purified intellects illuminated by divine light. The *perennial philosophy* framework — Brahmins, Pythagoras, Plato, Pseudo-Dionysius, and Scripture converging — is announced from the first pages of the text proper.
